title: Reflective Metastructure Q-plate for Ultrashort Laser Pulses
authors: Christopher G. O. Weiß, Bert Lägel, Benjamin Stadtmüller, Martin Aeschlimann, Tobias Eul
abstract: The orbital angular momentum of light is an intriguing property for developing light driven applications. It emerged as an independent degree of freedom by which to manipulate light and, consequently, the interaction of light with matter. Several methods exist for the generation of light carrying orbital angular momentum, mostly employing transmitting or reflecting optical components, which radially modulate the phase profile of the light. As one of such components, transmissive q-plates established themselves as standard elements due to their usability over a broad wavelength range. Here, we present our approach to build a highly reflective q-plate based on a plasmonic metasurface capable of converting orbital angular momentum from the nanostructure to ultrashort laser pulses without temporal broadening. We highlight its working principle over a wide range of wavelengths for reflection under normal and gracing incidence.
summary: The paper presents an experimental realization of a reflective Q-plate using a plasmonic metasurface made of gold nanorods. This device efficiently converts the spin angular momentum (SAM) of incident light into orbital angular momentum (OAM) upon reflection. Its significance lies in its ability to maintain high reflectivity and preserve the integrity of ultrashort laser pulses across a broad spectral range, making it ideal for advanced ultrafast optical applications.
main_problem: The goal is to develop a highly reflective Q-plate capable of converting orbital angular momentum (OAM) from nanostructures into ultrashort laser pulses without incurring temporal broadening.
main_result: The proposed plasmonic metasurface successfully achieves high reflectivity and broadband OAM generation for femtosecond pulses, overcoming dispersion issues associated with traditional transmissive Q-plates.
method: The authors fabricated a reflective metasurface using ordered arrays of gold nanorods on a dielectric spacer above a back reflector to mimic the phase transformation properties of conventional Q-plates upon reflection.
model_or_system: The physical system is a plasmonic metasurface constructed from anisotropic gold nanorods deposited on an $	ext{SiO}_2/	ext{Au}$-reflector stack. This structure leverages the geometric Pancharatnam-Berry phase acquired during reflection to couple input polarization state (SAM) into output OAM.
key_observables: High reflectivity ($>60\%$), generation of helical phase fronts ($\ell=\pm 1$) from circularly polarized light, and preservation of ultrashort pulse duration upon reflection.
important_parameters: Operating wavelengths range from $550 	ext{ nm}$ to $1000 	ext{ nm}$; incidence angles tested include $\alpha = 45^\circ$ and $\alpha = 5^\circ$; input pulses are in the femtosecond regime ($<30 	ext{ fs}$).
main_assumptions: The primary assumption is that the metasurface can effectively mimic the required birefringence landscape of conventional Q-plates while operating robustly under reflection geometry.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the device schematic, SEM images confirming nanorod dimensions and orientation, optical setups for characterization (reflectivity/beam profiles), and wavelength-dependent measurements showing OAM generation ($\ell=\pm 1$ donut modes) and polarization conversion.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the need for reflective Q-plates, details the plasmonic metasurface design using gold nanorods, presents experimental characterization across various wavelengths and incidence angles, confirms broadband operation, and discusses pulse integrity.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant to quantum optics and open quantum systems because it demonstrates a robust, solid-state platform for manipulating the angular momentum of light—a key resource in quantum information processing—using plasmonics rather than bulk optical elements.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Operating principle of the reflective plasmonic q-plate. Azimuthally oriented gold nanorods on a SiO2/Au-reflector stack on a Si-substrate impose a spatially varying geometric phase upon reflection under an angle of incidence α. The left panels show the phase front and polarization of the incident beam, whereas the right panels display the corresponding reflected states. Circularly polarized input light is converted into OAM-carrying beams with opposite helicity and ℓ= ±1, while linearly polarized input states give rise to radial and azimuthal vector beams with ℓ= 0.

Figure 2: Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images of the fabricated reflective plasmonic meta- surface q-plate based on arrays of gold nanorods on a SiO2/Au-substrate. (a) High-magnification SEM image of individual gold nanorods, indicating their typical dimensions (l = 200 nm, w = 85 nm). (b) SEM overview of the central region of the meta- surface showing the azimuthally varying nanorod orientations that implement the q-plate phase pro- file.

Figure 3: Schematic of the optical setup for char- acterizing the reflective q-plate. A tunable Ti:Sa- oscillator with an OPO provides femtosecond pulses from 540 nm to 1000 nm with pulse durations of 80 fs to 150 fs. The incident polarization is con- trolled by a quarter- or half-wave plate, and the metasurface (MS) is illuminated at α = 45◦or 5◦. (a) Reflectivity setup with power-meter detection. (b) Beam-profile setup with CCD-camera detection. For further analysis, either no additional optic, a polarizer, or a cylindrical lens can be placed in front of the camera.

Figure 4: Wavelength dependent OAM generation under 45◦illumination. (a) Beam profiles after reflection of σ+-light (s = +1) on the q-plate. (b) Beam profile from (a) with inserted cylindrical lens. (c) Beam profile after reflection of σ−-light (s = −1) with inserted cylindrical lens.

Figure 5: Wavelength dependent OAM generation under 5◦illumination. Beam profile after reflection of σ+-light (s = +1) on the q-plate for the wave- lengths 550 nm, 575 nm and 600 nm from left to right.
